w:r.w:t: Refined from C2A Transmutation Chain | v1.1
Make the probing zone smart — instead of making the whole robot expensive.

The Survey Robot is a compact autonomous rover with a self-leveling, 4-point stabilized, telescoping probe system that classifies buildability by combining local bracing signals, penetration data, neighborhood inference, and confidence-based adaptive surveying.

The Survey Robot solves the site qualification problem: determining, autonomously, which areas of raw undeveloped ground are safe to build on — without requiring a human geotechnical engineer at every site and without turning the robot into an overbuilt, expensive machine.

It does this by making the probing zone — not the whole robot — the intelligent part. Local ground-coupling replaces chassis mass. Swarm inference replaces individual sensor certainty. Adaptive convergence replaces brute-force uniform sampling.

Its output is a site heatmap: a confidence-scored spatial classification of every zone as BUILDABLE, MARGINAL, or REJECT. This heatmap is the gate that unlocks the rest of the House Brain build sequence.
Move to each survey point across raw terrain autonomously
Lower stabilizing foot frame and outer sleeve into ground
Actively equalize load across all 4 contact points before probing
Drive outer sleeve to shallow anchor depth — or log brace failure as data
Drive inner rod to target depth, recording continuous force-depth curve
Score each point as BUILDABLE / MARGINAL / REJECT with confidence level
Update site model using neighborhood evidence from surrounding points
Re-probe uncertain zone boundaries until confidence converges
Halt survey when new probes no longer meaningfully change the site model
Publish site heatmap to House Brain survey approval gate
Leave grade reference patch for Logistics Robot lane calibration
Nine constraints were identified and transmuted from first principles. Each transmutation was derived by analogy before any engineering solution was named.

Pushing a probe into compacted dry ground requires serious downward force. But the robot base is light. Instead of the probe going down, the rover slips, lifts, or tips — producing noisy resistance data.
Stop asking the whole robot body to resist probe force.
Place the probe centerline directly under the robot's center of mass — keeps force symmetric.
Add an outer sleeve / stabilizing collar that contacts the ground first.
The inner probe rod then travels through that stabilized guide.
Load path becomes: ground ↔ outer brace ↔ inner probe — not ground ↔ probe ↔ robot chassis.
Result: light rover + local ground-coupled probe fixture, not a heavy expensive rig.

The outer sleeve can stabilize the inner probe, but only if it contacts the ground cleanly. On uneven terrain or debris-covered surfaces, the sleeve may seat at an angle, rock during probing, or partially grip — producing bad data even though the bracing concept is correct.
Don't force the ground to be flat. Make the probe system adapt to the local angle.
Mount the outer sleeve on a self-leveling / compliant joint — not rigidly fixed to the chassis.
The robot's IMU reports chassis tilt. The sleeve actively adjusts to match local ground angle.
The sleeve seats flush regardless of the terrain angle under the rover.
Result: the sleeve becomes a small adaptive landing foot for the probe.

Even with a self-leveling sleeve, contact can be false: one edge on a rock, gravel under one side, or soft crust over weaker soil. The sleeve may appear seated, but under real load it settles suddenly, slips sideways, or rotates — corrupting the force-depth curve.
Don't let the sleeve rely on one contact ring alone.
Use a 4-point support frame around the center probe. The four feet touch down first.
Multiple contact points detect uneven support, redistribute load, and create a wider, truer base.
The 4-point frame converts unstable contact into distributed support.
Intelligence stays local to the probing zone — the whole rover does not need to become heavy.

A 4-point frame looks stable, but if one leg is barely touching and another is taking most of the weight, it can still wobble under probe load. The robot needs to know whether each foot is actually carrying load before committing to a full probe.
Don't just measure whether the 4 feet happen to share load. Make the robot force them to share load equally.
Each outer support leg has a small actuator or compliant preload mechanism.
A simple per-foot force sensor or motor-current proxy measures contact load.
A control rule equalizes contact force across all 4 points before probing begins.
The probe platform doesn't passively rest on the ground — it actively clamps into a stable stance.

Equal grip across 4 feet does not guarantee the shallow layer is strong enough to act as a brace. The robot can equalize perfectly on soft crust, loose sand, or brittle top layer — and the whole platform still sinks or drifts once the center probe adds deeper force.
Transmutation: Telescoping Load Path / Brace-With-Sleeve, Sense-With-Core
Separate the job of holding force from the job of sensing depth.
Outer sleeve / anchor tube goes in first and becomes the shallow structural brace.
Inner probe rod travels through the stabilized sleeve and does the actual sensing.
Load path from inner probe reaction closes through the outer sleeve into the ground — not back into the rover chassis.
Analogy: drive a stake first, then slide the measuring needle through the stake.
Result: deeper probe force is absorbed locally by the outer brace, not by the rover body.

A tent peg works great in firm ground. In loose sand or shallow fill, the peg enters but doesn't hold. The outer sleeve can enter weak near-surface soil but still provides no trustworthy reaction base for the deeper inner probe.
Transmutation: Brace Failure Becomes Soil Signal
If the shallow layer cannot support the outer brace, that itself is valuable survey data — not just a failure.
Shallow brace failure indicates: loose sand, weak fill, fractured crust, or poor near-surface support.
The robot does not say 'I failed.' It says: 'This location failed shallow bracing — that is a classification event.'
This feeds directly into site classification: REJECT, MARGINAL, or flag for alternate probe mode.
Result: weak shallow ground becomes an early hazard indicator that saves time, energy, and mechanism wear.

Shallow brace failure is valuable data — but the robot must know whether it means 'this point is genuinely bad ground,' or 'this is just a weak surface layer over something worth probing,' or 'relocate a meter and retry.' Treating all brace failures identically produces wrong classifications.
Transmutation: Neighborhood Inference Resolves Ambiguous Failures
Don't force certainty from a single failed probe point.
Log the shallow-failure signature, then probe adjacent points.
Use neighborhood patterns to infer whether the failure was a local surface anomaly or a broader weak zone.
One bad point surrounded by good points → local anomaly / marginal classification.
Cluster of bad adjacent points → weak zone / reject classification.
Mixed pattern → trigger denser sampling of that boundary.
This is standard geotechnical logic: subsurface investigations interpret patterns across points, not single readings.

Once the robot starts inferring from neighboring points, it needs rules for: how many nearby points are enough, how far 'nearby' means, when to trust interpolation, when to densify the survey grid, and when to stop. Without this the survey either under-samples or over-samples.
Transmutation: Confidence Convergence — Not Instant Certainty
The robot should not ask: 'Did this one point solve the ground?'
It should ask: 'What ground-model am I converging toward as more points come in?'
The survey has an endpoint in mind: a stable classification of the area with confidence scores per zone.
Each new probe updates the current belief — it does not restart from zero.
Analogous to Kalman filtering in autonomous vehicles: truth emerges from overlapping evidence.
Early points create a rough map. Later points refine edges. Ambiguous areas trigger denser sampling. Stable areas stop being sampled.

The robot now converges toward a site model as it probes. But it needs a stop rule: when is a zone confident enough? When do we stop probing a good area? When do we accept 'good enough' and move on? Without this the survey chases certainty forever and becomes too slow.
Transmutation: Stop When New Probes Stop Changing the Decision
Poke broad first — sparse initial grid across the full site area.
Find suspicious transitions — where zone classification changes between adjacent points.
Densify only where the story changes — probe tightly around uncertain boundaries.
Stop when new pokes in an area land in places that already feel like what surrounding pokes predicted.
The stop rule: 'Will the next probe change my build/reject decision for this zone?' If no — stop.
Survey ends when: new points mostly agree with local predictions, build/reject boundaries stop moving meaningfully, and extra points are unlikely to change the final placement decision.
The following improvements were added during spec refinement beyond the original transmutation chain.

The Survey Robot is the natural producer of the grade reference patch that the Logistics Robot needs to calibrate its lane conditioning system. This was not in the original spec — it was discovered as a cross-robot dependency during the logistics refinement.

At the conclusion of site qualification, the Survey Robot stamps one reference grade patch at the pre-selected staging zone entry point. This patch becomes the Logistics Robot's ground truth for all lane conditioning. No extra hardware required — the Survey Robot's existing 4-point foot frame can press a clean grade patch. The House Brain logs the patch coordinates as protected. Two robots share one hardware act.

The original spec treats partial penetration (inner probe stops before target depth) as a failure mode. In practice, the depth at which penetration resistance becomes too high is itself useful soil data — it tells you how thick the workable near-surface layer is.

Partial penetration is re-classified as a data event, not just a fault. The probe logs: depth at penetration resistance spike, force at spike, and whether resistance was gradual or sudden. Gradual = compacting layer, possibly workable. Sudden = rock, hardpan, or buried obstruction. This depth-to-resistance data feeds the site heatmap and can inform excavation depth planning for the Excavator Robot.
4.3 Moisture as Conditional Signal, Not Universal Fix

The transmutation chain explored whether wetting the soil around the outer brace improves shallow anchor hold. The answer is: sometimes yes, sometimes catastrophically no — and the robot needs to know which case it is in.

The robot does not wet soil by default. Instead, it carries a very small moisture-conditioning reservoir that is only activated when: (1) the initial penetration force curve suggests loose granular soil (which benefits from capillary tension), and (2) current moisture reading is below a threshold. Fine-grained soils, clays, and silts never receive moisture conditioning — these weaken under added water. The classification model determines soil class from the early probe curve before deciding whether conditioning is appropriate.

The original spec lists failure modes but doesn't resolve how the robot decides which failure is worth retrying vs. logging and moving on. Without this hierarchy the robot can waste time on unrecoverable site conditions.

Three-tier failure response: Tier 1 (retry once) — sleeve misalignment, single foot equalization failure, minor wheel slip. Tier 2 (log and move to adjacent point) — brace failure, partial penetration, shallow support unreliable. Tier 3 (log and skip zone) — terrain beyond mobility limit, standing water, repeated Tier 2 in a 3×3 area. Tier 3 clusters automatically become REJECT zones in the heatmap. The House Brain is notified after any Tier 3 event.
Cheapest robust outdoor mobility for survey task
Stable footprint; probe frame fits within wheelbase
Less chassis mass = less site disturbance before probing
No speed requirement; accuracy at probe point matters
No downtime for recharging during survey
No GPS dependency; works between structures and on any terrain
The outer sleeve is the bracing component. It is not the sensor. Its only job is to give the inner probe a stable, ground-anchored guide.
Diameter: 40 – 60 mm, hardened steel or carbide-tipped leading edge
Shallow penetration target: 200 – 400 mm — enough for reliable frictional hold in most soils
Self-leveling mount: passive gimbal or low-stiffness compliant joint, corrects up to ±12° from chassis tilt
4-point outer foot frame: four feet deploy radially around the sleeve base at 90° intervals
Per-foot actuators: small linear actuators equalize contact force across all 4 feet before probing
Per-foot load sensing: motor-current proxy or inline load cell — confirms equal grip
Brace failure flag: if equalization cannot be achieved within threshold, log brace_mode_failed and proceed to neighborhood inference
The inner rod is the sensing component. It travels through the stabilized outer sleeve and records the force-depth relationship that classifies the soil.
Diameter: 20 – 35 mm — fits through outer sleeve with clearance
Max probe depth: 2.0 – 3.0 m depending on soil and build requirements
Penetration force: up to 200 N — enough for most non-rocky soils
Load cell: inline, measures continuous penetration force at 10 Hz minimum
Depth encoder: tracks inner rod position to ±5 mm
Retraction: motorized, with stall detection if rod gets stuck
Tip: interchangeable — standard cone for normal soils, narrow spike for rocky/dry conditions

Optional sub-system. Small reservoir (200 – 500 mL) with gravity-fed ring dispenser around the outer sleeve base. Only activated when the probe classification model determines granular dry soil and current moisture is below threshold. Never used on fine-grained soils.
Chassis tilt detection; self-leveling sleeve correction
Eliminates GPS dependency on dense sites
Sleeve tilt relative to local ground plane

Design principle: every sensor serves the core classification task. No sensor exists purely for robot health monitoring — if a sensor breaks, the probe data degrades gracefully rather than the robot stopping entirely.
Consistent resistance increase with depth; no sudden spikes; sleeve braces cleanly
Moderate shallow resistance; some variability; sleeve braces with minor equalization
Brace failure, very low resistance throughout, sudden collapse, or standing water
Any brace failure = provisional REJECT; confirmed by adjacent points
Partial penetration, equipment fault, or contradictory signals
Triggers adjacent-point retry; escalates to Tier 3 if repeated
1 bad point surrounded by 4+ BUILDABLE points → reclassify as LOCAL ANOMALY / MARGINAL
3+ adjacent REJECT points → confirm as WEAK ZONE / REJECT; stop sampling that zone
Mixed BUILDABLE / REJECT boundary → trigger densified sampling (halve grid spacing) along boundary
UNKNOWN point → probe 2 adjacent points; use their curves to infer probable classification
Inference search radius: 1.5× grid spacing for initial survey, 0.75× grid spacing for densified zones
Phase 1 — Sparse grid: probe at standard spacing across full site polygon
Phase 2 — Boundary densification: halve grid spacing at every zone boundary with confidence < 0.75
Phase 3 — Anomaly resolution: probe 4 adjacent points around every UNKNOWN or isolated REJECT
Stop rule: survey ends when no zone boundary has moved meaningfully in the last 3 new probe points along that boundary, AND overall uncertainty score is below configured threshold
Override: House Brain can set a maximum probe count budget; robot optimizes point placement within budget
Generate initial sparse probe grid from site polygon; update route dynamically as classification proceeds
Execute sleeve deploy → foot equalize → sleeve seat → inner probe → retract sequence
Read IMU tilt; adjust sleeve mount angle to match local ground plane before deployment
Drive per-foot actuators until contact force is balanced across all 4 feet within threshold
Record continuous inner rod penetration curve at 10 Hz; detect stall and partial penetration events
Score each probe point as BUILDABLE / MARGINAL / REJECT / UNKNOWN with confidence level
Maintain live spatial site model; update zone classifications using surrounding points
Identify zone boundaries with confidence below threshold; generate densified probe points
Track whether new probes are meaningfully changing the site model; trigger stop rule
Classify each failure as Tier 1/2/3; decide retry, log-and-move, or log-and-skip
Execute grade reference stamp at staging zone entry point at survey completion
Publish all point records and live heatmap to House Brain in real time
Extend 4-point foot frame and equalize ground contact load
Run per-foot actuator equalization loop until balance achieved or flag failure
Drive outer sleeve to target shallow anchor depth
Drive inner rod to target depth; record force-depth curve
Execute full probe sequence at current position
Score current point from force-depth curve and sleeve status
Generate and execute densified probe grid around uncertain zone boundary
Execute grade reference stamp at current position for Logistics Robot
Publish current site classification map to House Brain
Stop current probe safely and log fault classification
Log as shallow failure signal; probe adjacent points; infer from neighborhood
Log depth and resistance spike type; use as depth-to-resistance data for heatmap
Log raw curve; flag for neighborhood confirmation
Moisture conditioning activated but grip unchanged
Proceed without conditioning; log soil response
Log as inaccessible; auto-classify as UNKNOWN; notify House Brain
Log as inaccessible; auto-classify REJECT or MARGINAL based on area context
Auto-classify zone as REJECT; stop sampling that zone; notify House Brain
Emit partial heatmap with uncertainty scores; House Brain decides proceed or extend
Static probe bench: probe rail, outer sleeve, inner rod, load cell, depth logging, 4-point support mockup
Prove stable repeatable force-depth curves from real soil samples
Footed probe platform: equalized support feet, self-leveling mount, brace/sleeve seating detection
Prove local stabilization logic on rough ground without rover
Rover integration: mount Phase 2 system on rover chassis with UWB navigation
Move, stop, deploy, probe, log, repeat autonomously
Classification engine: point classification, neighborhood inference, adaptive densification, stop rule
Produce real site heatmap decisions from multi-point surveys
House Brain integration: survey approval gate, site heatmap visualization, build zone placement, reference patch stamp
Full survey subsystem live in mission control; Logistics Robot receives grade reference
This is not a final BOM. The design intent is to keep every component as cheap as possible by concentrating intelligence in the probing zone rather than the robot body.
Cheap rover base — agricultural or industrial chassis, not custom
Simple probe mechanics — two concentric rods, not a full cone penetrometer rig
Motor-current proxy for load sensing — avoids dedicated force-torque sensors on each foot
No humanoid complexity — no arms, no legs, no balance systems
Local stabilization frame — makes the probing zone smart, not the whole machine heavy
Adaptive survey — stops early when certainty is sufficient; does not over-probe stable zones
Shared reference patch function — Survey Robot produces the Logistics Robot's calibration stamp at zero extra cost
Make the probing zone smart, not the whole robot expensive. Make failure informative, not just obstructive.
The Survey Robot is successful when it can:
Complete a full site survey autonomously without human intervention
Produce repeatable point classifications on the same soil type across different robots
Distinguish clearly weak zones from usable zones in a single survey pass
Convert probe failure into informative data rather than dead stops
Emit a site heatmap that the House Brain can use to place the build zone without additional human survey input
Avoid excessive oversampling by stopping when new probes stop changing the decision
Produce the grade reference patch for the Logistics Robot as part of the normal survey completion sequence
Operate correctly when individual sensors degrade — failing gracefully rather than catastrophically
House Brain Project | Survey Robot Blueprint v1.1 | Transmutation-derived architecture
